interactions.summary: Jiang reads and explains revolutionary texts around the Third Estate and the Declaration of the Rights of Man, presenting the nation and sacred rights as the new source of sovereignty.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang reading and interpreting revolutionary documents.

interactions.summary: He continues through the liberal rights framework: negative freedom, law as the community's will, government below the people, religious liberty, expression, and the new liberal order.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang reading and interpreting revolutionary documents.

claims.claim: The Third Estate pamphlet makes the people who had been politically nothing claim to be everything and demand representation.
claims.temporal_scope: Jiang's explanation of revolutionary pamphlet politics.

claims.claim: The Declaration of the Rights of Man begins modernity by treating rights as sacred, God-given, and prior to government.
claims.temporal_scope: Interpretation of the 1789 declaration in this lecture.

claims.claim: The modern liberal order rests on negative freedom, popular sovereignty, equality before law, religious tolerance, and freedom of expression.
claims.temporal_scope: Definition of the liberal order in this lecture.

signature_moments.moment: The nation becomes the new God.
signature_moments.source_phrase: we have a new God. It's a nation state
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This connects liberal modernity to religious substitution rather than simple secularization.

signature_moments.moment: Modern freedom is recast as negative freedom: do what you want unless you injure another or break the law.
signature_moments.source_phrase: this is a new idea of freedom. Negative freedom
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives the episode a precise political vocabulary before Robespierre attacks property.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Freedom to do what one wants so long as one does not injure others or violate law.
The new source of sovereignty in Jiang's reading of the Declaration, replacing king and church as supreme political authority.

chronology_notes.note: The transcript says the Bastille was stormed on July 14, 1789 and identifies July 14 as modern France's national day.
